resources.string: An offline encrypted photo gallery. Configured here, on your device — nowhere else.
Photo galleries are a worthwhile target — for data thieves, for forensic tools, and for anyone with brief access to your device. Encryption keeps the content yours.
Works fully offline. The app declares no INTERNET permission.
No account, no sign-up, no cloud. The vault lives in app-private storage.
Master key is generated and held by your device’s secure hardware.
Asking Android Keystore where it can place the master key.
Master key will live in a dedicated security chip — separate from the main processor. Best case for this device.
Master key will live in TrustZone — isolated from normal OS memory, on the same chip as the main processor. Strong protection.
This device has no secure hardware available. The key would be protected by the OS only. You can still proceed, but the threat model is weaker — physical access to an unlocked device is enough.
Couldn’t reach Android Keystore (%1$s). Setup will continue and try again when the master key is created. See logs in dev panel.
Here’s how your photos are protected on this device.
Industry standard with hardware acceleration on this device. Protects against all classical attacks — and is already quantum-resistant.

Your stack is already quantum-resistant. Data is AES-256-GCM, the password runs through Argon2id, and the key lives in the hardware StrongBox — all symmetric cryptography. Grover’s quantum algorithm only halves its strength, leaving AES-256 at ~128-bit, beyond any brute force. There is no quantum-vulnerable asymmetric cryptography (the kind Shor’s algorithm breaks) in the vault, so “harvest now, decrypt later” does not apply to a local vault.
How will you unlock the gallery?
Minimum 8 characters. Letters of any language, digits, symbols, mixed case — all fine. Highest entropy per character.
6–12 digits. Faster to type one-handed. Smaller alphabet, compensated by stricter Argon2id at unlock time.

Either choice is fine. PIN uses 128 MiB / 5 iterations of Argon2id, password mode uses 64 MiB / 3 iterations — so the KDF makes brute-force comparable across the two. What differs is your typing speed.
Pick a password — at least 8 characters. Letters of any language, digits, symbols, mixed case — all fine.
PIN must be at least 6 digits
PIN must be at most 12 digits
Password must be at least 8 characters
Type your secret again to confirm
Enter your current secret, then a new one. Your photos are not re-encrypted — only the vault key is re-wrapped.
%1$d more wrong attempts will wipe the vault.
Always on. The wait grows with each failed attempt — typos cost a second, repeated guessing locks the app for half an hour.
Destroys the vault if too many guesses fail in a row.
Wiping deletes ALL encrypted files and the master key. There is no recovery, even if you remember the secret afterwards.
After how many consecutive wrong attempts should the vault be wiped? (1–999)
Your vault is ready. Here\'s what you chose:
This device exposes no secure hardware. The master key is protected by the OS only — physical access to an unlocked device is enough to compromise it.
